topics.title: Overcorrection of chronic hyponatraemia & ODS
highYield: Correction limit in chronic hyponatraemia: ≤8 mmol/L per 24h; ≤6 mmol/L if high ODS risk.
High ODS risk: Na⁺ ≤105, hypokalaemia, alcohol use, malnutrition, advanced liver disease.
Overcorrection rescue: stop hypertonic saline, give 5% dextrose ± desmopressin 2–4 µg to re-lower Na⁺.
ODS is delayed (2–6 days) and often biphasic: initial improvement then deterioration.
topics.explainer:
Osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS) is driven by **too-rapid correction**, not by the absolute sodium. In chronic hyponatraemia the brain has extruded osmolytes to adapt, so a fast rise in serum Na⁺ pulls water out of neurons.

Safe correction is **≤8 mmol/L in 24h** (≤6 if high risk). If you overshoot, the intervention is *proactive re-lowering* with 5% dextrose and desmopressin — don't just wait.

Classic exam trap: a question gives an acute symptomatic patient (seizure) where a rapid initial 4–6 mmol/L bump with hypertonic saline is correct, and expects you to *still* respect the 24h ceiling.
pitfalls: Applying acute-hyponatraemia bolus logic to the 24-hour ceiling.
Forgetting desmopressin can be used deliberately to prevent overcorrection.

flashcards.front: Maximum Na⁺ correction over 24h in chronic hyponatraemia?
flashcards.back: ≤8 mmol/L (≤6 mmol/L if high ODS risk).

flashcards.front: Name three high-risk features for ODS.
flashcards.back: Na⁺ ≤105, hypokalaemia, alcoholism, malnutrition, advanced liver disease (any three).

flashcards.front: How do you rescue an overcorrected patient?
flashcards.back: Stop hypertonic saline; 5% dextrose infusion ± desmopressin 2–4 µg to re-lower Na⁺.

topics.title: Distal (type 1) renal tubular acidosis

encounters.note: Correct but slow — confused urine pH threshold

highYield: Type 1 (distal) RTA: inability to acidify urine → urine pH >5.5 despite systemic acidosis.
Hypokalaemic, normal anion gap metabolic acidosis; positive urine anion gap.
Complications: nephrocalcinosis, calcium phosphate stones, bone disease.
Causes: Sjögren's, amphotericin B, hereditary, hypercalciuria.
topics.explainer:
Distal RTA is a failure of H⁺ secretion in the collecting duct (α-intercalated cells). Because you cannot excrete acid, urine pH stays inappropriately high (**>5.5**) even when the blood is acidotic.

The picture is a **normal anion gap (hyperchloraemic) metabolic acidosis with hypokalaemia** and a **positive urine anion gap** (Na + K − Cl), signalling low urinary ammonium. Alkaline urine plus hypercalciuria and low citrate drives **calcium phosphate stones and nephrocalcinosis**.

Contrast with type 2 (proximal): urine can acidify (pH <5.5) once the lowered bicarbonate threshold is reached, and Fanconi features may coexist.
pitfalls: Mixing up type 1 (urine pH stays >5.5) with type 2 (can drop <5.5).
Forgetting the urine anion gap is positive in distal RTA.

flashcards.front: Urine pH in distal (type 1) RTA during acidosis?
Stone/calcification type associated with distal RTA?

topics.title: Primary membranous nephropathy & PLA2R

encounters.note: Chose immediate immunosuppression regardless of risk

highYield: Anti-PLA2R antibodies positive in ~70–80% of primary MN; useful for diagnosis and monitoring.
Risk-based therapy: low-risk → supportive/ACEi for up to 6 months first.
High-risk (falling GFR, very high proteinuria, high anti-PLA2R): rituximab, or cyclophosphamide + steroids (Ponticelli).
Secondary causes: malignancy, hepatitis B, SLE (class V), NSAIDs — screen age-appropriately.
topics.explainer:
Primary membranous nephropathy is an autoantibody disease; the **M-type phospholipase A2 receptor (PLA2R)** on podocytes is the dominant target antigen (THSD7A in a minority). Anti-PLA2R titres track disease activity and can guide treatment and predict remission.

Management is **risk-stratified**. Many patients remit spontaneously, so low-risk disease gets **conservative therapy** (RAS blockade, diuretics, statin, anticoagulation if albumin very low) for up to 6 months. Immunosuppression — **rituximab** or the **Ponticelli regimen** — is reserved for high-risk or deteriorating patients.

Exam trap: reflexively starting immunosuppression in a stable low-risk patient. Always screen for secondary causes.
pitfalls: Immunosuppressing low-risk patients who may remit spontaneously.
Forgetting malignancy screening in older patients with MN.

flashcards.front: Dominant target antigen in primary membranous nephropathy?
flashcards.back: PLA2R (phospholipase A2 receptor); THSD7A in a minority.

flashcards.front: First step for LOW-risk primary MN?
flashcards.back: Supportive therapy (RAS blockade etc.) for up to 6 months before considering immunosuppression.

topics.title: IgA nephropathy — MEST-C & risk
highYield: Oxford MEST-C score: Mesangial, Endocapillary, Segmental sclerosis, Tubular atrophy, Crescents.
Foundation of care: maximal supportive therapy (RAS blockade, BP <120 systolic, SGLT2i).
Persistent proteinuria >0.75–1 g/day despite optimisation → consider systemic steroids (weigh TESTING trial toxicity).
Newer agents: targeted-release budesonide (Nefecon), sparsentan.
topics.explainer:
IgA nephropathy is the commonest primary glomerulonephritis worldwide; galactose-deficient IgA1 immune complexes deposit in the mesangium. The **Oxford (MEST-C)** classification adds prognostic histology.

The pillar of treatment is **optimised supportive care**: RAS blockade to maximal tolerated dose, tight BP control, and increasingly **SGLT2 inhibitors**. Systemic corticosteroids are considered for persistent proteinuria despite this, but the **TESTING** trial highlighted infection risk, so it's a careful risk–benefit call. **Nefecon** (gut-targeted budesonide) and **sparsentan** are newer options.
pitfalls: Jumping to steroids before supportive therapy is maximised.
Overlooking SGLT2 inhibitors as part of the supportive backbone.

flashcards.front: What does the C add to the Oxford MEST score?
Proteinuria threshold prompting consideration of steroids in IgAN?
flashcards.back: Persistent >0.75–1 g/day despite maximal supportive therapy.

topics.title: Haemodialysis adequacy — Kt/V & URR

encounters.note: Confused single-pool vs equilibrated Kt/V target

highYield: Minimum target single-pool Kt/V ≥1.2 per session for thrice-weekly HD; aim ~1.4.
URR (urea reduction ratio) target ≥65%.
Kt/V = clearance × time / volume of urea distribution (≈ total body water).
Inadequate dosing drivers: access recirculation, short time, low blood/dialysate flow, clotting.
topics.explainer:
Dialysis adequacy is quantified by **Kt/V** (dialyzer clearance K × time t, divided by urea distribution volume V) and by the simpler **urea reduction ratio (URR)**.

For conventional thrice-weekly HD, aim for a **single-pool Kt/V ≥1.2** (target ~1.4) and **URR ≥65%**. Equilibrated Kt/V runs slightly lower than single-pool because of post-dialysis urea rebound. If numbers fall short, hunt for **access recirculation, reduced treatment time, low flows, or clotting**.
pitfalls: Confusing single-pool with equilibrated Kt/V targets.
Treating Kt/V as the only marker of a good dialysis prescription (ignores fluid, mineral, symptoms).

flashcards.front: Minimum single-pool Kt/V for thrice-weekly HD?

topics.title: Antibody-mediated rejection (AMR)

encounters.note: Missed C4d and DSA as the AMR triad

highYield: AMR diagnosis (Banff): histologic injury + C4d peritubular capillary staining + donor-specific antibodies (DSA).
C4d-negative AMR exists if molecular/microvascular inflammation criteria are met.
Treatment: plasma exchange + IVIG ± rituximab; consider steroids.
Contrast with T-cell-mediated rejection (tubulitis, interstitial infiltrate) → steroids ± ATG.
topics.explainer:
Antibody-mediated rejection is driven by **donor-specific antibodies (DSA)** against HLA. The classic **Banff triad** is: histological evidence of acute tissue injury (microvascular inflammation — glomerulitis + peritubular capillaritis), **C4d deposition** in peritubular capillaries, and **circulating DSA**.

Modern Banff criteria allow **C4d-negative AMR** when molecular or microvascular inflammation thresholds are met. Treatment centres on antibody removal and suppression: **plasma exchange + IVIG**, often with **rituximab**. This is distinct from **T-cell-mediated rejection** (tubulitis/interstitial infiltrate), which responds to pulsed steroids ± ATG.
pitfalls: Forgetting DSA is required to call AMR.

flashcards.front: Name the classic Banff triad for AMR.
flashcards.back: Tissue injury (microvascular inflammation) + C4d in peritubular capillaries + donor-specific antibodies.

topics.title: CKD-MBD: phosphate & the FGF23 axis
highYield: Earliest biochemical change in CKD-MBD is a rise in FGF23, before overt PO4 or PTH change.
FGF23 lowers phosphate (phosphaturia) but also suppresses calcitriol → drives secondary hyperparathyroidism.
KDIGO: lower elevated phosphate toward normal; avoid routine calcium-based binders in adults.
Treat modifiable factors first: dietary phosphate, dialysis adequacy, then binders, then calcimimetics/active vitamin D.
topics.explainer:
In CKD-MBD the **first measurable change is a rise in FGF23**, well before phosphate or PTH move. FGF23 promotes phosphaturia to defend serum phosphate, but it also **suppresses 1-α-hydroxylase**, cutting calcitriol and thereby fuelling **secondary hyperparathyroidism**.

KDIGO guidance is to **lower elevated phosphate toward the normal range** using diet, adequate dialysis, and binders — while **restricting calcium-based binders** to limit vascular calcification. Active vitamin D and calcimimetics address the PTH axis.
pitfalls: Naming PTH rather than FGF23 as the earliest change.

flashcards.back: Rise in FGF23 (precedes phosphate and PTH changes).
Promotes renal phosphate excretion; suppresses calcitriol synthesis.

topics.title: Contrast-associated AKI — risk & prevention
highYield: Main preventive measure with evidence: peri-procedural isotonic IV crystalloid in at-risk patients.
PRESERVE trial: no benefit of N-acetylcysteine or sodium bicarbonate over saline.
Highest risk: pre-existing CKD (especially with diabetes), volume depletion, high contrast volume.
Modern intra-arterial data suggest true CA-AKI risk is lower than historically feared.
topics.explainer:
Contrast-associated AKI risk has been **revised down** in recent years, with much historical 'contrast nephropathy' reflecting confounding. The intervention with the best evidence remains **peri-procedural volume expansion with isotonic saline** in higher-risk patients.

The **PRESERVE** trial showed **no added benefit** from N-acetylcysteine or sodium bicarbonate over saline alone. Highest risk sits with **CKD plus diabetes, volume depletion, and large contrast loads** — so hydrate, minimise contrast, and avoid other nephrotoxins around the procedure.
pitfalls: Recommending N-acetylcysteine or bicarbonate as beneficial.
Withholding necessary imaging from stable eGFR patients out of exaggerated fear.

flashcards.back: Peri-procedural isotonic IV saline in at-risk patients.

flashcards.front: What did the PRESERVE trial show?
flashcards.back: No benefit of N-acetylcysteine or sodium bicarbonate over saline.
